prompt:
I just spent a session rewriting the ETRO essay content. The
`src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` file has the new blurb,
scope statement, and section bodies I wanted. But the live site still
shows the old content. The file was saved successfully, no errors. What's
going on, and what should the workflow have been so I don't run into
this every time we do content work?
response: I now have everything I need. The ENG-204 entry in the feedback log is a near-exact match, and the data-flow principle from `docs/engineering/debugging.md §4.1` and the CMS source-of-truth principle in `docs/engineering.md §0` complete the picture. Let me compose the full diagnosis. --- ## Diagnosis The content you edited is **dead code on the live site.** The file `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` is a **seed/migration route** - a one-time HTTP trigger that, when called, writes content into the Payload CMS database. Editing the TypeScript source of that route does absolutely nothing to what the site renders, because the site never reads from that file. It reads from the **database**, which still holds the old content from the last time (or the only time) the route was ever called. --- ## Root Cause This is an instance of the **"dead write"** pattern: content was modified in the seed route file but the route was never invoked to push those changes into the database, so the database - the actual source of truth - was never updated. The project has a documented precedent for exactly this failure: **ENG-204** from `docs/engineering-feedback-log.md`: > *"Elan image placeholder section kept reappearing after CMS deletion — seeding route `update-elan/route.ts` still defined `imagePlaceholders` in the first content block, overwriting CMS deletions on every call. Removed from source and re-seeded."* That incident is the mirror image of yours: ENG-204 had a seed route **overwriting** desired CMS state. You have a seed route **sitting unexecuted** while the CMS holds the old state. The shared root cause is the same: **the seed route file and the database are two separate stores, and they only sync when the route is explicitly called.** The data-flow principle that makes this visible is stated in `docs/engineering/debugging.md §4.1`: > *"Data sync issue — the UI reads from a different source than what was modified."* And the master principle from `docs/engineering.md §0`: > *"Single source of truth for data. When the same data exists in multiple files, there must be an automated sync mechanism — not human discipline."* --- ## Fix ### Step 1 - Call the route to push your edits into the database With the dev server running on port 4000 (verify first per `docs/port-registry.md`): ```bash curl -s -X POST http://localhost:4000/api/update-etro | python3 -m json.tool ``` If the route uses `GET`, substitute accordingly. Confirm the response contains your new blurb, scope statement, and section bodies - not a generic `{ ok: true }`. Then verify the live page picked up the changes: ```bash curl -s http://localhost:4000/ | grep -o '[^<]*\|<p>[^<]*' | head -20 ``` Or open the page in a browser and confirm the new text is visible (per `guardrail-engineering-10`: HTTP 200 is not sufficient, check the browser). ### Step 2 - Audit the route for residual stale content Open `src/app/(frontend)/api/update-etro/route.ts` and verify the full document it writes is exactly what you want. In particular, check: - The blurb field - The scope statement field - Every section body Any field not explicitly set by the route will be left as whatever the database already has. Any field set to a hardcoded old value will **overwrite** your good CMS edits with stale seed content (the ENG-204 failure mode in reverse). ### Step 3 - Deploy After verifying on localhost, promote to production. The database on production is separate from local. Either: - Call the route against your production URL, or - Re-enter the content via the Payload admin panel at `https://yilangao.com/admin` (which is the authoritative edit path going forward - see Correct Workflow below) --- ## Correct Workflow for Content Work This is the architectural mismatch you should avoid permanently: | What you did | What the site reads | |---|---| | Edited `update-etro/route.ts` | The database | Seed routes are **write-once bootstrapping tools**, not a content editing interface. There are two correct workflows depending on what you're doing: ### For day-to-day content edits (right tool) Use the **Payload admin panel** directly: 1. Go to `http://localhost:4000/admin` (or the production admin URL) 2. Find the ETRO global or collection document 3. Edit the blurb, scope, and section bodies in the Lexical editor 4. Save - the database is updated immediately and the frontend reflects it on next render Or use the **inline-edit interface** on the frontend if it's wired up (the `EditableText` / `EditButton` components noted in `cms-parity/SKILL.md`). ### For bulk/programmatic content operations (seed routes) If you need a seed route: 1. Edit the route file with the new content 2. **Immediately call the route** - the file edit is only a staging area 3. Verify the database was updated: `curl http://localhost:4000/api/globals/<slug> | python3 -m json.tool` 4. Check the rendered page in a browser The rule of thumb: **the route file is a migration script, not the content store.** Treat it exactly like a SQL migration: writing the file is not running the migration. --- ## Principle **`docs/engineering.md §0` - Single Source of Truth:** The database is the source of truth for CMS content. A seed route file that has never been called is not in the data flow at all - it is inert. Any content editing session that modifies a seed route file without triggering it produces zero visible change and no error, which makes this failure mode particularly hard to notice. The correct mental model: the Payload admin panel and inline-edit surface write directly to the source of truth; seed routes are a one-way pipe that must be opened deliberately.
